title: Extractive QA (Span Extraction)
concept: Extractive QA predicts the start and end indices of the answer directly within the context text, commonly solved using BERT.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of Extractive QA?

options.text: Extracting a specific span of text from a document

exercises.explain: Extractive QA works by picking out a specific segment (or span) of text from a given document.

cards.title: Find the Answer!
cards.body: Have you ever taken an open-book quiz where you could find the exact answer written right there on page 5? AI can do the exact same thing using a technique called Extractive QA!

cards.title: What is Extractive QA?
cards.body: Extractive QA (Span Extraction) is a task where a model reads a question and a context paragraph, then extracts the exact answer directly from the paragraph. Instead of generating new words, it identifies the start and end positions (indices) of the answer.

cards.title: The Highlighter Trick
cards.body: Imagine reading a book and using a yellow highlighter to mark the exact sentence that answers a question. You aren't writing any new words in the margins; you are just highlighting a start and end point of text that is already there. That's exactly how Extractive QA works!

cards.title: A Real Example
cards.body:
Context: "The Great Wall of China was constructed over many centuries, starting in the 7th century BC."
Question: "When did construction of the Great Wall begin?"

An Extractive QA model will output the start and end indices pointing to the exact span: "7th century BC".

cards.title: It's Not Creative Writing
cards.body: Myth: Extractive QA models can write creative, conversational answers like ChatGPT.\nFact: They cannot create new words! They can only copy and paste a direct snippet of text (a span) that already exists in the document you gave them.

cards.title: Extracting vs. Generating
cards.body: Let's look at the difference between extracting an answer and generating a brand new one.

left.text: Cuts out "1969" directly from the sentence: "Apollo 11 landed on the moon in 1969."

right.text: Writes: "Humans first walked on the lunar surface during the summer of 1969."

exercises.prompt: What happens if the answer to the question is not present in the provided context text?

options.text: It will write a creative guess based on its own imagination.
It will rewrite the context to include the correct information.
It will fail to extract a correct span (or output an empty span).
It will search the web automatically.

exercises.explain: Because Extractive QA can only highlight text that is already there, it cannot answer questions if the information is missing from the provided context.

exercises.prompt: True or False: BERT can be used for Extractive QA by predicting the start and end positions of the answer.
exercises.explain: BERT and similar models are commonly trained to predict the start and end tokens of the answer span for Extractive QA.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of how Extractive QA works.
exercises.sentence: Extractive QA models locate the answer by predicting the start and end [blank] of the text span.
exercises.explain: By finding the start and end indices, the model knows exactly where the answer begins and ends in the document.

exercises.prompt: Which of these answers looks like it was generated by an Extractive QA model?

options.text: "Based on the provided context, the Golden Gate Bridge was completed in 1937 after four years of construction."
"I think it was built in 1937, but I would need to check a history book to be absolutely sure."

exercises.explain: AI models designed for span extraction output index markers (start and end) pointing to the existing text, whereas generative models write free-form sentences from scratch.

exercises.prompt: How does a BERT model actually locate and extract an answer from a document in Extractive QA?

options.text: It translates the question into another language to find the answer.
It predicts the start and end positions of the answer in the text.
It writes a brand new paragraph summarizing the answer.
It searches the entire internet for matching web pages.

exercises.explain: Extractive QA models like BERT only predict the start and end position of the answer within the input text. They cannot generate new words or change the phrasing.
